name: Resilience Framing vs. Sovereignty Framing
consequences: Positioning platform-neutral access as a continuity-of-service and supplier-concentration risk (rather than privacy ideology) makes the case legible to procurement and contingency-planning stakeholders who control budget allocation and standards-setting. However, this reframing risks depoliticizing the underlying democratic accountability concern and may inadvertently legitimize half-measures that reduce platform dependency on paper while preserving structural lock-in through alternative monopolies or opaque fallback mechanisms.
options: Anchor all advocacy and feasibility reporting in Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's stated 2026–2029 public-sector resilience commitments, supplier-diversification language, and continuity-planning frameworks, treating platform-neutrality as a concrete risk-mitigation requirement rather than a sovereignty aspiration.

Lead with geopolitical and supply-chain continuity arguments targeting defense, health, and tax authorities separately, framing platform-neutral access as essential for operational resilience during policy disputes, sanctions regimes, or commercial terms changes that could lock Denmark out of critical services.

Develop a paired narrative emphasizing both citizen digital rights and institutional autonomy, explicitly connecting platform lock-in to democratic accountability risks, then measure coalition response to determine whether dual-framing strengthens or fragments stakeholder alignment.
review: The tension lies between making the case politically viable versus preserving the underlying structural critique. All three options treat resilience as instrumental framing, but none directly addresses the risk that a successful 'resilience' intervention could result in a fallback system that is merely slower or less convenient without actually reducing platform vendor control over trust signals or distribution channels.

name: Demonstrator Scope: Lean Proof-of-Concept vs. Feature Parity
consequences: Keeping phase two demonstrators explicitly non-production and standards-based (FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or similar) makes them defensible as research artifacts and avoids feature creep that would consume budget and timeline. The trade-off is that demonstrators will lack the institutional polish, integration maturity, and user-experience refinement that procurement decision-makers unconsciously expect, creating a perception gap that incumbent operators can exploit by claiming alternatives are 'not ready for production.'
options: Build one mobile demonstrator using only open standards (FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect) against a simulated Danish public-sector identity provider on GrapheneOS, and one hardware-token or browser-based fallback demonstrator, both explicitly labeled as research artifacts with published architectural and trust-model documentation, accepting that they will look less polished than commercial MitID.

Develop a thin wrapper layer that allows the GrapheneOS demonstrator to authenticate against a subset of actual Danish banking or public-sector test environments (not live MitID, but production-adjacent), trading phase-two scope for higher credibility and earlier detection of integration friction points that will inform phase-three procurement language.

Create two parallel demonstrators—one lean standards-based prototype and one feature-complete reference implementation covering user onboarding, recovery workflows, and integration patterns—explicitly marketing the first as proof-of-concept and the second as a reference for procurement vendors, accepting the higher phase-two cost in exchange for fewer excuses from incumbent operators to dismiss the work as incomplete.
review: The core tension is between defensibility as research and credibility as a harbinger of deployable alternatives. Even the leanest demonstrator will be compared implicitly to MitID's maturity, and incumbent operators will frame any gap as proof that alternatives cannot meet Danish institutional standards. None of the options directly address the hidden cost of rebuilding institutional trust workflows that MitID has had years to optimize, meaning demonstrators may prove technical feasibility while obscuring implementation friction that would emerge in real procurement.

name: Coalition Building: Broad Civil Society vs. Narrow Institutional Leverage
consequences: Engaging civil-society organizations, privacy advocates, academics, and Folketinget committee members creates political visibility and normative pressure, but diffuses advocacy focus across constituencies with competing priorities (privacy, digital rights, competition, consumer protection). Narrow institutional engagement with procurement and standards bodies (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, AltID operator, MitID board) is more legible to decision-makers but requires navigating existing power relationships and risks being compartmentalized into advisory rather than decision-making roles.
options: Build a formal multi-stakeholder coalition (civil society, academia, security researchers, consumer advocates, contingency-planning authorities) with monthly working groups and public position statements, accepting that coordination overhead will consume phase-one effort and that diverse stakeholder priorities may dilute the core supplier-concentration message.

Establish a small core advocacy team (3–4 respected security and policy experts with Danish institutional standing) to directly engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID governance bodies, and AltID procurement leads, explicitly seeking formal advisory roles or working-group membership before attempting broader civil-society mobilization.

Pursue a parallel path: embed one coalition member in each of three institutional gatekeepers (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, AltID operator, relevant Folketinget committee) as either an advisory consultant or working-group participant, while simultaneously running a public-facing civil-society campaign to create external pressure that legitimizes institutional insiders' advocacy for platform-neutrality language.
review: The tension is between political legitimacy and institutional access. Broad coalition-building generates public pressure that can overcome inertia, but creates surface area for incumbent operators to criticize the project as advocacy-driven rather than evidence-based. Narrow institutional engagement accelerates decision-making but risks being contained as a technical advisory input rather than shaping actual procurement requirements. The options do not address the fundamental misalignment: institutional gatekeepers (especially MitID operator) have strong incumbent incentives to avoid supplier-neutral requirements, and neither broad nor narrow coalitions can force formal adoption without political or regulatory mandate.

name: Standards Intervention Timing: AltID Procurement Window vs. EU Digital Identity Wallet Alignment
consequences: Denmark's AltID rollout (expected finalization by 2027–2028) creates a narrow window for embedding platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into procurement and design specifications. Simultaneously pushing platform-neutrality language into EU Digital Identity Wallet standards and specifications could amplify pressure on Danish procurement by making non-compliance with European interoperability norms visible. The trade-off is that EU-level standards work is slow, diffuse, and often symbolic, while the AltID window is fixed; misallocating effort toward EU work could leave Danish procurement uninfluenced.
options: Focus phase-three effort entirely on AltID-related procurement documents, standards-setting bodies, and design decisions, treating EU Digital Identity Wallet engagement as a secondary output only if AltID traction is demonstrable by month 16 and bandwidth permits.

Run parallel tracks: dedicate 40% of phase-three effort to AltID procurement and design intervention, and 60% to European Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and eIDAS2 implementation guidance, betting that EU-level language on platform neutrality and resilience will create normative and regulatory pressure that flows back into Danish AltID decisions.

Sequence interventions so that phase-three begins with intensive AltID procurement engagement (months 17–20), and if institutional traction is weak by month 20, pivot to EU standards bodies and publication-focused activities for months 21–24, accepting that this leaves limited time for EU advocacy but preserves the option to reallocate without sunk effort.
review: The core tension is between tactical focus on the near-term AltID decision and strategic alignment with longer-term European regulatory momentum. AltID procurement is visible, bounded, and has real decision-making power; EU standards work is diffuse and often produces non-binding guidance that incumbent platforms can ignore. However, AltID operates within EU frameworks, and language in eIDAS2 implementation guidance or European Digital Identity Wallet specifications could either unlock or foreclose Danish procurement options. None of the options address the risk that AltID procurement may already be locked in by the time the project begins formal engagement, rendering the entire intervention moot.

name: Fallback Authentication Mechanism: Hardware Token vs. Browser-Based vs. Hybrid Approach
consequences: A hardware-token-based fallback (e.g., FIDO2 security keys or smartcards) reduces mobile-OS dependency entirely but introduces physical distribution, device-compatibility friction, and citizen adoption barriers for vulnerable populations. Browser-based fallback (web-only authentication, hardware-token support through USB or NFC) preserves citizen accessibility but still depends on operating-system or browser-vendor choices and does not eliminate all platform lock-in. Hybrid approaches (multiple fallback mechanisms with different trust roots) increase implementation complexity and certification burden but provide resilience against any single mechanism becoming unavailable.
options: Design a hardware-token-based fallback authentication path (FIDO2 security keys or national smartcard infrastructure) that operates independently of mobile or desktop operating systems, accepting citizen friction around physical token distribution, loss/replacement workflows, and reduced convenience for remote authentication, in exchange for maximal platform independence.

Develop a browser-based fallback that supports both USB-connected hardware tokens and software-based FIDO2 attestation across major browsers, treating the browser vendor layer as a less critical chokepoint than the mobile OS duopoly, while acknowledging that this approach still depends on browser and OS ecosystem cooperation.

Construct a three-tier fallback architecture—hardware token as primary fallback, browser-based WebAuthn as secondary, and a secured in-person or counter-service authentication path for citizens unable to use the first two—accepting significant complexity and certification overhead in exchange for maximal resilience across demographics and scenarios.
review: The core tension is between platform independence and citizen accessibility. A pure hardware-token fallback is technically clean but creates exclusion risks for citizens without literacy or physical dexterity to manage tokens, and for public-sector institutions without token-distribution infrastructure. Browser-based alternatives are more accessible but do not eliminate platform lock-in; they merely move it one layer up. The options implicitly assume that a fallback mechanism, however well-designed, can coexist with MitID as a parallel system without Danish institutions and citizens gravitating back to the more convenient MitID path, undermining the fallback's actual deployment and utility.

name: Institutional Overhead and Security Review: Internal Capability vs. External Credibility
consequences: Conducting technical security review and compliance assessment internally minimizes cost and timeline but risks producing work that incumbent operators or skeptical regulators dismiss as self-interested or insufficiently rigorous. Engaging external security auditors, EU-recognized assessment bodies, or independent laboratories adds cost (estimated 300k–500k DKK) and delays but produces credible third-party validation that procurement decision-makers and standards bodies will weight more heavily. The hidden trade-off is that external reviewers may impose certification or assurance requirements that are expensive and time-consuming, potentially derailing phase-two demonstrator timelines.
options: Conduct internal security architecture review and threat modeling for demonstrators using the project team's expertise, supplemented by external peer review from respected Danish and European security researchers at no cost, publishing findings with full transparency and accepting institutional and incumbent skepticism about independence.

Engage a recognized European cybersecurity assessment body or digital-identity-qualified auditor to perform formal security review of demonstrator architecture and threat models (partial phase-two budget allocation), producing a credible third-party assessment that carries weight in procurement discussions and standards bodies.

Build a tiered review approach: internal security architecture review in phase two, followed by a formal external audit of selected demonstrator components in phase three (conditional on phase-two results), deferring some external validation cost until results justify the investment, accepting the risk that phase-three procurement language will lack fully certified third-party backing.
review: The tension is between cost and credibility in a regulatory environment where incumbent operators will immediately question the independence and rigor of any external alternative. External audits increase cost and timeline risk but produce artifacts (certificates, formal audit reports) that procurement processes require. However, even formal external audit does not guarantee that procurement decision-makers will weight platform-neutral recommendations equally with incumbent vendors' established certification portfolios and institutional relationships. The options do not address whether demonstrator-level security review will satisfy regulatory bar for production deployment, which is likely much higher and may render phase-two credibility gains moot.

name: Exit Scenario and Phase-Three Scope Flexibility: Pivot to Standards vs. Procurement Hold
consequences: The project plan already acknowledges that if formal AltID traction is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input. This flexibility preserves project value even if Danish procurement influence fails, but it also creates a perception risk: if the project pivots to 'standards advocacy' after institutional engagement disappoints, it signals that Danish procurement remains closed, potentially undermining the project's credibility with future decision-makers. Conversely, maintaining an aggressive procurement focus through month 24 despite weak signals risks consuming final-phase budget on institutional inertia rather than producing outputs of lasting value.
options: Establish a formal decision gate at month 16 with explicit criteria for 'institutional traction' (e.g., formal written engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, documented inclusion of platform-neutrality language in draft AltID specifications, or confirmed participation in AltID procurement working groups). If two of three criteria are unmet, phase three pivots to publication, coalition consolidation, and EU standards bodies; if two or more are met, procurement focus continues through month 24.

Commit to full 24-month procurement engagement with AltID and related bodies regardless of formal signals, treating institutional inertia as a cost of participation rather than a failure signal, and planning phase-three outputs (demonstrators, feasibility report, policy proposal) as foundational evidence that will inform future identity-infrastructure decisions even if immediate AltID adoption is unlikely.

Allocate phase-three budget equally across three parallel tracks from months 17–24: AltID procurement engagement (30%), EU Digital Identity Wallet and eIDAS2 implementation guidance (40%), and publication, coalition maintenance, and follow-on-project planning (30%), accepting reduced focus on any single track in exchange for institutional flexibility and multiple paths to influence.
review: The core tension is between optimism about institutional change and pragmatism about regulatory capture and incumbent resistance. A hard decision gate at month 16 is cleaner and conserves resources, but risks signaling that Danish digital identity is structurally closed to platform-neutral alternatives, potentially discouraging future advocates. Unconditioned 24-month AltID focus consumes budget fighting institutional inertia. The balanced three-track approach distributes risk but creates coordination overhead and may dilute impact across all three channels. None of the options confront the underlying political reality: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID governance bodies do not perceive supplier-concentration risk as a priority by month 16, the project cannot manufacture that priority through technical or advocacy effort alone.

name: Trust Model Anchoring: Platform Integrity Signals vs. Institutional Certification
consequences: The project can anchor fallback authentication to institutional cryptographic custody (Danish public-sector PKI) rather than relying on Apple or Google device-integrity attestations. This shifts the trust burden from platform vendors to the state's own certification authority, making the fallback path formally independent. The trade-off is that institutional custody requires secure key management, audit trails, and potentially slower deployment cycles compared to delegating trust to consumer-platform APIs.
options: Anchor the fallback authenticator directly to the Danish state's existing digital-signature infrastructure by issuing hardware-bound certificates through Statens IT, using existing NSIS Substantial-level PKI and removing the platform integrity dependency entirely.

Establish a hybrid model where the fallback path uses institutional PKI for non-repudiation while device integrity signals from Apple or Google remain optional supplementary signals, allowing graceful degradation if platform APIs become unavailable or restricted.

Create a new independent Danish Digital Identity Trust Authority with its own cryptographic root, issuing device-neutral certificates to citizens via secure enrollment (in-person or video-verified), decoupling the fallback path from both MitID and platform vendors.
review: The core tension is between institutional speed-to-deployment and the weight of new governance overhead. All three options assume the Danish public sector has the appetite and capability to operate or sponsor an independent trust anchor, but institutional PKI projects are notoriously slow (18–36 months for governance alignment alone). The options miss a simpler intermediate step: using an existing third-party institutional certification body (such as a Danish bank or telecom operator with existing NSIS credentials) to bridge the gap, avoiding full state custody while still achieving independence from platform vendors.

name: Demonstrator Visibility and Stakeholder Expectations: Open-Source Publication vs. Restricted Access vs. Staged Disclosure
consequences: Early publication of demonstrator code signals technical feasibility to international audiences and removes secrecy liability, but also invites product-deployment pressure from civil-society groups and media coverage that conflates a proof-of-concept with a ready-to-deploy alternative. Restricted access preserves control and allows iteration without external pressure, but creates perception of opacity that undermines the credibility argument. Staged disclosure balances evidence with managed expectations.
options: Publish all demonstrator code as open-source under a permissive license by month 14, with accompanying 'Not for Production Use' guidance, explicitly inviting academic and security-research critique and establishing the codebase as a reference implementation for future procurement language.

Restrict demonstrator access to an invited consortium of Danish government agencies, security researchers, and EU standards bodies until month 20, then release only anonymized architectural documentation and threat-model analysis without exposing implementation details that might be misused or prematurely deployed.

Release a staged series of technical white papers (months 12, 16, 20) describing architectural patterns and security proofs without publishing code, while offering source-level walkthroughs to credentialed institutional stakeholders (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, Rigsrevision, MitID operator) under NDA.
review: The tension is between credibility-through-transparency and control-over-narrative. Open-source publication builds research legitimacy but invites unrealistic production expectations and media oversimplification. Restricted access maintains control but feeds accusations of secretive influence-peddling. The options miss a critical audience-segmentation insight: civil-society groups and security researchers need different communication products (code and specifications) than procurement officers (risk matrices and vendor-neutral requirements language), so a single disclosure strategy fails both.

name: Fallback Path Integration Scope: Standalone Authenticator vs. MitID Interop Layer vs. AltID-Specific Design
consequences: A standalone fallback authenticator (FIDO2 over WebAuthn or hardware token) is easiest to prototype and hardest to mandate into existing production systems. An interoperability layer that bridges MitID and platform-neutral authentication extends the technical scope significantly but increases perceived relevance to incumbent operators. Designing specifically for AltID's stated architecture reduces scope but couples the project's success to AltID's timeline and willingness to incorporate feedback.
options: Develop a wholly independent fallback authenticator using FIDO2/WebAuthn and hardware-security-key support, compatible with generic OpenID Connect identity providers but not integrated with MitID, demonstrating that users can authenticate to Danish public services without platform-store mediation.

Engineer a thin OpenID Connect middleware layer that allows MitID to remain the primary authenticator while transparently routing authentication requests through a platform-neutral channel (such as a user-controlled hardware token or desktop application) when the mobile-app path is unavailable or blocked.

Co-design the fallback mechanism to align explicitly with the published AltID architecture roadmap, including API compliance, eIDAS2-wallet integration points, and Substantial-level assurance requirements, making the demonstrator a reference implementation for AltID's contingency-access pathway.
review: The core tension is between technical independence and institutional relevance. Standalone design is cleanest but looks like a parallel system rather than a fix to the actual risk. AltID-specific design is most institutionally relevant but surrenders the project to AltID's timeline and political priorities; if AltID stalls or resists, the demonstrator becomes orphaned. The options miss the pragmatic intermediate approach: design for generic OpenID Connect and NSIS Substantial level, then map that design to AltID's interfaces during phase three only if AltID momentum exists, rather than pre-committing the demonstrator architecture to AltID requirements.

name: Security Review Timing and Audience: Early External Assessment vs. Deferred Institutional Audit vs. Peer Review Publication
consequences: Early security review by an internationally recognized firm (month 10–12) provides credibility before demonstrators are complete, but consumes budget and may surface design changes that delay phase-two output. Deferring review until phase three allows demonstrators to stabilize, but weakens the credibility claim for phase-two disclosure and removes independent validation from the evidence base. Published peer review is lowest-cost but offers no confidential feedback and subjects work to academic debate rather than institutional confidence-building.
options: Commission a formal security assessment by a tier-one third-party firm (such as Trail of Bits or a equivalent European consultancy) in month 10, covering threat modeling, FIDO2/WebAuthn protocol validation, and eIDAS Substantial-level compliance, delivering a public summary report that lends institutional credibility to phase-two demonstrators.

Conduct an internal security-design review by the project's senior mobile-security researcher in month 8, documented as a white paper, then invite ad-hoc feedback from Danish government CISO and relevant EU standards bodies without commissioning a full external audit until phase three.

Publish the demonstrator security analysis as a peer-reviewed research paper (target: IEEE Security & Privacy or equivalent) in parallel with phase-two development, using publication acceptance as the external credibility marker rather than hiring external consultants.
review: The core tension is between external credibility and budget allocation. Early third-party review strengthens phase-two messaging but costs 300–500 kEUR and may require rework. Deferring leaves no independent validation in the evidence base for procurement discussions. Published peer review is cheap but slow (8–12 months to review and publication) and does not address procurement officers' need for applied security assurance rather than academic merit. The options miss a hybrid approach: conduct a rapid internal threat-modeling workshop with invited government security leads in month 9 (low-cost, high-stakeholder engagement), document the findings as a working paper for institutional circulation, then commission external review only if phase-two feedback indicates the need.

name: Coalition Composition and Advocacy Messaging: Privacy-First Narrative vs. Business-Continuity Risk vs. Democratic Sovereignty Framing
consequences: Privacy-first messaging attracts civil-society and academic audiences but is easily dismissed by procurement officers as ideological. Business-continuity risk (supplier concentration, service disruption, cost opacity) resonates with government risk managers and auditors. Democratic sovereignty appeals to politicians but invites nationalist backlash and weakens EU collaboration. Each framing leads to different coalition partners, allies, and ultimately different procurement language.
options: Lead with platform-dependency as a supplier-concentration and business-continuity risk, centering the coalition on Danish government auditors, public-sector CIOs, risk-management bodies, and EU resilience authorities, with messaging focused on service continuity and vendor lock-in rather than privacy or sovereignty.

Build a broad cross-constituency coalition spanning privacy advocates, security researchers, civic-tech organizations, and sympathetic parliamentarians, using decentralized-identity and user-agency language to frame the issue as citizen control and digital rights, accepting that this messaging is less directly actionable in procurement but builds political pressure.

Frame platform-neutral access as a precondition for democratic digital governance, emphasizing that essential public services should not depend on commercial platform policies or geopolitical platform restrictions, targeting messaging at government digital-policy committees, EU member-state coordinators, and national digital-sovereignty working groups.
review: The core tension is between procurement relevance and coalition breadth. Business-continuity framing is most actionable but narrow; it excludes civil-society and academic allies who care about privacy or democratic principles. Privacy and sovereignty framings are broader but less directly linked to procurement decision-making, so they risk becoming background noise in procurement documents. The options miss a dynamic messaging strategy: use business-continuity language in government and procurement contexts, privacy language in EU and academic contexts, and sovereignty language in parliamentary and national-strategy contexts, coordinating across audiences rather than committing to a single frame.

name: AltID Engagement Entry Point: Direct Stakeholder Proposal vs. EU Standards Influence vs. Procurement Consultation Participation
consequences: Direct engagement with the AltID program office (months 10–14) offers the clearest path to influence but requires buy-in from an incumbent operator (likely Nets or a state agency) who may view platform-neutral requirements as disruptive. EU-level standards influence (eIDAS2 working groups, ETSI, digital-identity consortia) is more diffuse but may eventually flow down to national procurement as harmonized requirements. Procurement-consultation participation occurs late (month 18+) but directly shapes tender language if the project is invited.
options: Approach the AltID program office in month 9 with a formal proposal for platform-neutral fallback integration, positioning the project as a risk-mitigation and compliance partner, and seek a formal collaboration agreement or reference-implementation role that gives the project direct input into AltID architecture decisions.

Engage with the eIDAS2 technical working groups and the European Digital Identity Wallet governance bodies (through the project's EU-level contacts) to introduce platform-neutrality and resilience requirements into draft interoperability standards, using the project's demonstrators and research as evidence that these requirements are technically feasible.

Identify and join the formal public-consultation processes for AltID and related procurement (typically months 14–18) by direct engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, submitting detailed technical feedback and mapping the project's demonstrators to the stated procurement requirements.
review: The core tension is between directness and institutional palatability. Direct AltID engagement is fastest but depends on incumbent operator receptiveness; if AltID leadership perceives the project as a threat or unwelcome scrutiny, doors close quickly and cannot be reopened. EU-level engagement is slower and more diffuse but generates external pressure that's harder to ignore. Procurement-consultation participation is straightforward but arrives late (month 18+), leaving little time for feedback iteration or design changes. The options miss an earlier, lower-stakes entry point: engage the MitID operator's own risk or compliance team (month 8) on the supplier-concentration risk itself, positioning the project as a partner in addressing a recognized vulnerability rather than as an external critic of their platform choices.

name: Phase-Three Scope Contingency: Procurement-Centered Pivot vs. Standards-And-Publication Focus vs. Deferred Implementation Roadmap
consequences: If AltID engagement is weak by month 16, pivoting phase three entirely toward EU standards input and publication preserves credibility and impact but abandons direct influence over Danish procurement. A procurement-centered pivot requires identifying an alternative procurement vector (healthcare, tax, or local-government identity systems) and shifts timeline and scope. A deferred roadmap documents what would be needed for future implementation, creating a standing proposal for the next identity-infrastructure cycle without claiming immediate impact.
options: If AltID collaboration is not formalized by month 15, reallocate phase-three budget toward publication of a comprehensive platform-neutral-identity reference architecture (targeting publication by month 22), EU standards-body engagement, and a consolidated policy brief for the next government digital-strategy cycle, treating procurement influence as a longer-term outcome.

Identify an alternative procurement path (such as healthcare authentication for the electronic patient record system or municipal ID-verification for welfare services) that is scheduled for mid-2027 or later, and redirect phase-three effort toward building demonstrator evidence and procurement language for that system rather than pursuing a weakening AltID engagement.

Document a detailed multi-year implementation roadmap for platform-neutral Danish digital identity, including institutional governance, PKI requirements, legacy-system integration patterns, and cost-benefit analysis, positioning the project as a strategic input to the next round of government digital-infrastructure planning (2027–2030) rather than claiming delivery within the current 24-month window.
review: The core tension is between maintaining momentum and acknowledging institutional inertia. A full pivot to standards and publication is graceful but may feel like failure to government stakeholders who funded the project expecting domestic procurement influence. Identifying an alternative procurement vector is pragmatic but extends timeline and requires political capital to build coalition support in a new domain. A deferred roadmap is intellectually honest but risks being shelved as academic work without follow-through accountability. The options miss the most politically viable path: by month 15, conduct a rapid (2-week) internal decision meeting with Danish government counterparts to jointly assess AltID feasibility; if prospects are weak, jointly agree to a reduced phase-three scope (e.g., focused standards work and coalition consolidation) that the government explicitly endorses, turning the contingency into a collaboratively re-scoped outcome rather than a unilateral project retreat.

name: Demonstrator Platform Provenance: GrapheneOS Reference vs. Downstream Hardening vs. Minimal Baseline Fork
consequences: Choosing a single well-known open-source Android fork (GrapheneOS) provides immediate credibility and avoids building custom firmware, but ties the project's technical narrative to a specific vendor's reputation and security posture, which could shift or attract platform-specific criticism from MitID operators. A minimal fork approach offers maximal independence but requires significantly more engineering effort and introduces new attack surface that reviewers will scrutinize.
options: Execute both demonstrators exclusively on unmodified GrapheneOS, documenting the platform's existing security model and integrity controls to establish equivalence to Apple/Google app-integrity signals.

Develop a minimal, formally-verified Android baseline fork focusing only on the authentication stack (bootloader, app signing, notification channels) while inheriting core OS security from AOSP and subjecting it to a dedicated threat-modeling exercise.

Partner with a second-source Android device manufacturer (such as Fairphone or a privacy-focused OEM) to run the demonstrator on their upstream firmware, creating evidence that platform-neutral fallback is achievable outside the duopoly without custom forks.
review: The core tension is between credibility-through-reuse (GrapheneOS) and independence-through-custom-work (minimal fork). The options miss the risk that any Android-based approach will face push-back from MitID operators who will claim that device integrity cannot be verified without Apple/Google's proprietary secure enclave or MDM integration, regardless of open-source alternatives; a hardware-token demonstrator alongside the Android path would neutralize that objection more effectively than choosing between Android variants.

name: Coalition Representation Balance: Academic Legitimacy vs. Activist Credibility vs. Corporate Neutrality
consequences: Visibly leading with academic researchers and university affiliation provides institutional credibility that procurement officials respect, but may signal impotence or academic distance to incumbent operators and activist stakeholders who expect direct pressure and public advocacy. Conversely, heavy activist or civil-society visibility energizes public support and media attention, but risks marginalizing the project as ideological rather than resilience-focused in Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's eyes.
options: Center the project's public voice and decision-making around a university-based security research group with advisory input from civil-society partners and discrete engagement with sympathetic Folketinget members, positioning the work as a technical-governance initiative.

Establish a formal steering committee with equal representation from academic researchers, privacy advocates, business-continuity and security consultants, and procurement specialists, publishing all steering decisions and meeting notes to signal transparency and balance competing stakeholder interests.

Cultivate selective corporate partnerships (e.g., with Danish or Nordic fintech companies, banking consortia, or insurance firms exposed to continuity risk) as counterweight to incumbent operators, framing platform-neutral fallback as essential infrastructure rather than ideological choice.
review: The core tension is between institutional credibility (needed to influence procurement) and public legitimacy (needed to withstand incumbent pressure to delay or defund). The options miss the timing problem: if public activism begins before demonstrators are published, incumbents will preemptively label the project as hostile and lobby for phase-three defunding; if academic positioning persists too long without public resonance, the project loses coalition momentum and becomes isolated from civil-society constituencies that actually vote and shift public discourse.

name: MitID Operator Engagement Cadence: Early Co-Design vs. Late-Stage Review vs. Parallel-Path Avoidance
consequences: Approaching the MitID operator (Nets or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's direct contractor) early for co-design or feedback establishes collaboration framing but signals that the project seeks permission, weakening leverage in phase three when procurement language is written. Deferring MitID engagement until after demonstrators are published avoids premature capture but risks being perceived as adversarial, triggering formal non-cooperation and incumbent counterarguments that the project misunderstands MitID's architecture.
options: Schedule a formal information-sharing session with the MitID operator in month 3 to present the resilience framing and feasibility roadmap, explicitly requesting feedback on architectural constraints and inviting them to co-author risk-mitigation language for AltID procurement documents.

Conduct demonstrator development and publication entirely without MitID operator input, then present the published feasibility report and working code as a fait accompli to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, forcing a choice between defending the status quo or acknowledging the platform-neutral alternative as technically credible.

Engage the MitID operator only through Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, framing all technical questions as requests for official interpretation of eIDAS2 conformance, creating a formal delegation chain that prevents the operator from claiming the project bypassed proper institutional channels.
review: The core tension is between collaboration (which risks normalization of incumbent objections) and independence (which risks formal institutional opposition). The options miss the leverage point: MitID operator fears around reputational damage and forced redesign are strongest only if AltID procurement language becomes non-negotiable first; engaging too early creates negotiation leverage for the operator to dilute requirements; engaging too late wastes opportunities to convert technical concerns into co-authored design principles that the operator cannot later disown.

name: External Security Review Sequencing: Third-Party Assessment vs. Peer-Led Academic Audit vs. Deferred Official Certification
consequences: Commissioning an independent third-party security review of the demonstrators signals rigor and produces credible evidence for procurement language, but external reviews cost 0.5–1.0 million DKK and require 8–12 weeks, consuming phase-two timeline and budget that might be needed for scope contingencies. A lighter peer-review model through university networks costs less but lacks the institutional weight that procurement authorities expect.
options: Allocate dedicated budget in phase two to commission a formal security audit from an external firm with eIDAS compliance expertise, producing a published assessment report that can be cited in AltID procurement consultations and RFP language.

Establish a peer-review working group comprising security researchers from other Nordic countries and EU digital-identity programs, conducting collaborative threat modeling and design review on a volunteer or low-cost basis, publishing results as academic papers rather than formal certifications.

Defer formal external review until phase three, after demonstrators are published and initial AltID procurement engagement has occurred, then commission a focused audit only if procurement officials explicitly request third-party validation as a condition for including platform-neutral language.
review: The core tension is between early institutional credibility (external review) and late-stage budget flexibility (deferred review). The options miss the risk that procurement authorities may treat an unreviewed demonstrator as proof-of-concept only, not as evidence sufficient to justify design changes, making the review effectively mandatory rather than optional; conversely, commissioning review too early before demonstrator scope is fully defined risks audit findings that force disruptive rework in a compressed timeline.

name: AltID Rollout Alignment Strategy: Direct Requirement Injection vs. Interoperability Standards Lever vs. Contingency Procurement Trigger
consequences: Attempting direct inclusion of platform-neutral fallback language in AltID's official design documents requires political leverage and momentum that may not be available until phase three, and facing incumbent resistance from operators already contracted to deliver the current plan. A standards-based approach (pushing eIDAS2 or NSIS working groups to endorse platform neutrality) is slower but creates formal regulatory pressure that AltID must eventually absorb; a contingency trigger approach (framing fallback access as a continuity requirement only if AltID deployment delays or fails) avoids head-to-head conflict but may never activate.
options: Initiate a formal request to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen in month 20 for inclusion of platform-neutrality and certified-fallback requirements in the AltID system architecture and procurement RFP, leveraging published demonstrators and coalition testimony to pressure acceptance before RFP freeze.

Engage with eIDAS2 and NSIS technical working groups through Danish government and EU digital-identity programs to establish platform-neutrality and resilience as standardized design principles, allowing AltID to adopt these as compliance requirements rather than treating them as project-specific demands.

Propose a formal contingency authentication trigger that activates fallback-path deployment only if AltID experiences defined delays (e.g., more than 6 months beyond planned schedule) or if platform-availability incidents affect critical public services, positioning the project's work as a risk-management layer rather than an alternative architecture.
review: The core tension is between direct leverage (which requires momentum but delivers faster results) and standards pressure (which is slower but harder to dismiss as special-interest advocacy). The options miss the procurement-timing problem: AltID's architecture is likely already frozen or in advanced RFP preparation by month 16–18, making direct requirement injection impossible unless the project achieves extraordinary political traction; a contingency framing avoids procurement conflict but essentially admits defeat on phase-three procurement goals, converting the project into a long-term insurance policy rather than an active intervention.

name: Fallback Authentication User Experience Framing: Bureaucratic Friction vs. Digital-Native Simplicity vs. Context-Conditional Activation
consequences: A fallback authentication path that requires explicit user choice, biometric re-enrollment, or hardware-token distribution will face adoption friction and incumbent arguments that it increases complexity and operational cost. Designing for seamless fallback (automatic activation if primary path fails, pre-enrolled credentials) reduces user friction but increases infrastructure and security-assurance complexity, requiring more extensive testing and review.
options: Design the fallback path as an explicit opt-in mechanism that users must consciously enroll and activate through a separate portal, accepting bureaucratic friction as a necessary cost to isolate fallback credentials and reduce attack surface for the majority of users who remain on the primary iOS/Android platform.

Integrate fallback credential enrollment into initial onboarding of all citizens (similar to recovery codes in banking), treating platform-neutral access as a default capability rather than an alternative, with seamless failover if platform-specific authentication becomes unavailable.

Implement a context-conditional activation model where fallback authentication is automatically offered only to users in specific risk cohorts (e.g., public-sector employees, security-critical roles, or citizens in regions with documented platform-outage history), reducing organizational overhead while creating a visible user base with real fallback dependency.
review: The core tension is between adoption simplicity (frictionless fallback) and security-assurance clarity (explicit opt-in). The options miss the trust-model inversion risk: any fallback path that is too convenient will face regulatory and operational challenge from incumbent operators arguing that ease of fallback undermines the integrity model they built for the primary platform, because users may migrate to fallback for trivial reasons, fragmenting the trusted user base; conversely, an explicitly friction-laden fallback path will be dismissed as unfit for mass deployment even in contingency scenarios.

name: Publication and Disclosure Pacing: Staged Transparency vs. Full Early Release vs. Embargo Until Phase Three
consequences: Publishing feasibility reports, demonstrator code, and threat models as work proceeds (staged transparency) builds coalition momentum and public scrutiny that strengthens procurement advocacy, but also alerts incumbent operators to project details early, giving them time to develop counterarguments and lobbying strategies. A full early release (month 10) accelerates public discourse but maximizes exposure to incumbent delay tactics; an embargo until month 20+ protects phase-two development but risks losing media and stakeholder attention.
options: Publish the phase-one feasibility report openly, then release demonstrator code and architecture documentation on a rolling basis as components mature, maintaining a public blog and monthly progress updates to sustain coalition visibility and media attention throughout the project.

Maintain comprehensive project documentation and demonstrators in a private repository during phase one and two, releasing everything simultaneously in month 15–16 as a single comprehensive publication event (code, reports, threat models, test results), creating maximum media impact and compressed timeline for incumbent response.

Publish the feasibility report in month 8 under a university press and open-access protocol, but place demonstrator code and technical architecture details under a controlled-release regime until month 18, shared only with verified stakeholders (government bodies, security researchers, coalition members) to prevent premature incumbent countermeasures.
review: The core tension is between public momentum (staged disclosure) and strategic timing (embargoed release). The options miss the coordination problem: publication timing that serves coalition-building goals does not align with procurement-cycle timing, because AltID RFP windows and decision gates are fixed; a feasibility report published in month 8 will have lost political salience by month 18 when procurement decisions actually occur, requiring the project to continuously regenerate attention through media and advocacy, which is resource-intensive and vulnerable to incumbent counter-narrative campaigns.
